title: Can God Become Man? The Incarnation Question
subtitle: Whether the hypostatic union is even a coherent possibility, weighed against Tawhid's absolute transcendence
summary: Beyond whether Jesus claimed to be God lies a harder question: could God become man at all, even in principle? This article presents the classical Christian doctrine of the hypostatic union and kenosis in its strongest form, the Islamic objection from Tawhid that such a claim is a category error rather than merely an unproven one, and explains why both metaphysical positions are internally coherent on their own starting premises.

seo.metaTitle: Can God Become Man? The Incarnation, Hypostatic Union, and Tawhid
seo.metaDescription: Is the Incarnation coherent? Examine the hypostatic union and kenosis (John 1:14, Philippians 2:6-8) against Islam's Tawhid objection (Quran 112:3, 19:35).

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body:
Christians and Muslims already disagree about whether Jesus ever said, in so many words, "I am God." That is a historical and textual question, addressed in this library's companion article, Did Jesus Claim to Be God? This article asks a different, harder question: setting the historical evidence aside, is the Incarnation — God becoming a man — even a coherent thing to claim, on its own terms?

Classical Christian doctrine answers yes, using two ideas drawn from its own scripture. The first is the hypostatic union: in the person of Jesus Christ, one person subsists in two complete natures, fully God and fully man, "without confusion, without change, without division, without separation," as the Council of Chalcedon put it in 451 CE. The second is kenosis, or self-emptying, from Philippians 2:6-8, where Christ, "existing in the form of God," is said to have "emptied himself, taking the form of a servant." On the classical reading, this is not a later patch invented to rescue an awkward doctrine; the vocabulary comes from the New Testament itself.

Islamic theology answers no — and not merely "not proven." Starting from Tawhid, the absolute oneness and utter transcendence of God, Muslim theologians hold that a Creator who is by definition without body, without need, without beginning, and without partner cannot also be a body that is born, that hungers, that suffers, and that dies, in the very same subject. On this view the Incarnation is not an extraordinary miracle awaiting evidence; it is closer to a category error, the mixing of two kinds of being — Necessary Existence and contingent existence — that cannot be mixed. The weight of this objection in Islam is tied to the concept of shirk, associating a partner with God, which the Quran treats as uniquely serious.

This article lays out both positions in their strongest, most carefully reasoned form, and ends without declaring a winner: the real disagreement lies further back, in what each tradition holds God's nature to allow in the first place.

sections.title: A Different Kind of Question
sections.body:
Most of the difficult questions in this library are historical or textual: what does a given verse say, when was a given text written, what did a given prophet actually teach. The question of this article is different in kind. It is a question of metaphysics — the branch of philosophy concerned with what kinds of things can exist and how they can be related to one another.

Put simply: even if every Gospel passage said exactly what Christian theology claims it says, and even if that reading could be established beyond dispute, would the claim itself — that the eternal, uncreated Creator of the universe became, without ceasing to be that Creator, a specific finite human being born in a specific place at a specific time — be a coherent claim to make about anything called "God"? Or does the very idea contain a hidden contradiction, regardless of what the texts say?

This is worth separating from the historical question addressed elsewhere in this library, because the two arguments are independent of each other. A reader could conclude that the Gospels do present Jesus claiming divinity, and still ask whether that claim is metaphysically possible. A reader could conclude that the Gospels do not present such a claim, and still find the philosophical question worth examining in its own right, since Christian theology, whatever its textual basis, asserts the Incarnation as an actual event, not a poetic figure of speech.

Both the classical Christian doctrine and the Islamic objection to it have been developed by careful, technically sophisticated theologians and philosophers over many centuries. Neither side holds its position naively. What follows presents each in its strongest form, on its own terms, before comparing them.

sections.title: The Difficulty, Stated Plainly
sections.body:
State the difficulty as sharply as its most serious critics state it. Classical theism — the broad view of God shared, in outline, by Jewish, Christian, and Muslim philosophers alike — holds that God is a necessary being (He cannot fail to exist), eternal (without beginning or end), immutable (not subject to change), infinite, and radically unlike anything created. A human being, by contrast, is a contingent being (one that might not have existed), temporally bounded, mutable, finite, and unlike God in exactly these respects.

The doctrine of the Incarnation asserts that one and the same subject is, simultaneously and permanently, both of these things: necessary and contingent, immutable and yet subject to growth and change, infinite and yet an infant nursing at his mother's breast, all-knowing and yet, in his own words, not knowing "that day or that hour" (Mark 13:32). Stated this baldly, the objection is not that this is unusual or unproven; the objection is that it looks like asserting a straightforward contradiction — that some subject is both a certain way and not that way, in the same respect, at the same time.

This is essentially how many Muslim theologians frame the problem, and it deserves to be taken exactly this seriously rather than dismissed as a failure to understand Christian doctrine. From the standpoint of Tawhid, the issue is not primarily that the Gospels fail to say this clearly (Muslims also make that separate, historical argument elsewhere); it is that no amount of additional evidence could rescue a claim that is incoherent on its face. A being that is by definition uncreated cannot, without contradiction, also be a being that is created — even if the claim is attached to only "part" of a composite subject, since the composite subject as a whole would then be both.

Christian theology has never treated this objection as trivial. It is precisely why it took the Church several centuries, and a series of major councils, each correcting an error the previous one had left unresolved, to state the doctrine of the Incarnation in a form its own theologians considered logically defensible. That development is the subject of the next section.

sections.title: How Each Tradition Arrived at Its Position
sections.body:
The classical Christian formula did not appear all at once. After the Council of Nicaea (325 CE) settled that the Son is fully divine — homoousios, "of the same essence," with the Father — the Church spent over a century working out how that full divinity related to Christ's full, equally insisted-upon humanity, correcting proposals on both sides that seemed to solve the coherence problem too easily.

Apollinaris of Laodicea, in the fourth century, proposed that in Christ the divine Word simply replaced the human rational mind — solving the unity problem by reducing Christ's humanity to a body without a human mind. This was condemned at the Council of Constantinople (381 CE), on the reasoning that if any part of human nature was not truly assumed by Christ, that part could not be considered healed or redeemed by him; his humanity had to be complete. Nestorius, Patriarch of Constantinople in the fifth century, was understood by his opponents to teach the opposite error — two separate persons, divine and human, loosely joined in Christ — which was condemned at the Council of Ephesus (431 CE) for dividing Christ into two subjects rather than one. Eutyches then proposed that Christ's humanity was absorbed into his divinity, becoming, in effect, one nature only after the union — a view condemned at the Council of Chalcedon (451 CE) for erasing the reality of Christ's ongoing humanity.

Chalcedon's own formula, the Chalcedonian Definition, tries to state the boundary conditions precisely rather than explain the underlying mechanism: Christ is "acknowledged in two natures, without confusion, without change, without division, without separation; the distinction of natures being by no means taken away by the union, but rather the property of each nature being preserved, and concurring in one Person and one Subsistence." This is the technical meaning of the hypostatic union: one hypostasis (person), two physeis (natures), united without mixture or division. Chalcedon does not claim to make the union fully comprehensible; it claims to state, negatively, every way the union must not be misdescribed, having watched each of those misdescriptions fail in turn.

Islamic theology's engagement with this question runs on a largely separate track, since the Quran, revealed in the seventh century in a milieu that included various Christian communities in Arabia, addresses the nature of Jesus directly and, from the Islamic point of view, conclusively, without needing to await or respond to Chalcedon's technical vocabulary. What later Muslim theologians (the mutakallimun) added, over centuries of contact and formal disputation with Christian scholars, was a rigorous philosophical vocabulary of their own for stating why the Incarnation fails: the distinction between the Necessary Existent (wajib al-wujud), who exists by His own nature and cannot not exist, and contingent existents (mumkin al-wujud), who depend on something else for their existence and could have failed to exist. Classical scholars such as Ibn Taymiyyah devoted lengthy, detailed treatises — most notably Al-Jawab al-Sahih li-man Baddala Din al-Masih ("The Correct Answer to Those Who Have Corrupted the Religion of Christ") — to engaging Christian doctrine on its own theological and philosophical terms, arguing that no formulation of the hypostatic union, however carefully worded, avoids attributing origination, need, and change to the God of Tawhid.

Both traditions, then, arrived at their positions only after sustained internal debate and refinement, not by asserting the first formula that came to mind.

sections.title: Tawhid: Why the Incarnation Is a Category Error
sections.body:
The Quran's objection to the idea that God could take, or become, a son is stated with unusual force and repetition, and it flows directly from Tawhid, the doctrine of God's absolute oneness explored fully in this library's companion article, What Is Tawhid? Tawhid is not simply the claim that there is numerically one God rather than two or twelve; it is the claim that God's nature admits no division, no composition, no change, no need, and no likeness to created things. God is described in the Quran as as-Samad, the Self-Sufficient Sustainer on whom all else depends and who depends on nothing, and the Quran states elsewhere that nothing whatsoever resembles Him.

Surah Al-Ikhlas, the Quran's most concentrated statement on the nature of God, addresses the question of divine sonship directly:

لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ

"He neither begets nor is born," (Quran 112:3, Saheeh International)

The verse denies two things at once, and both matter here: God does not beget, which rules out a literal divine son proceeding from Him, and God is not begotten, which rules out God Himself having an origin — precisely the point at issue in the Incarnation, since to "become" a human being born of Mary at a particular moment in time is, on this reading, to be begotten into history in some sense.

Surah Maryam, named for the mother of Jesus and containing the Quran's most sustained narrative of his birth, turns to the same theme immediately after affirming Jesus's own honored status as "the word of truth" spoken by God:

مَا كَانَ لِلَّهِ أَن يَتَّخِذَ مِن وَلَدٍۢ ۖ سُبْحَٰنَهُۥٓ ۚ إِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًۭا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ

"It is not [befitting] for Allah to take a son; exalted is He! When He decrees an affair, He only says to it, \"Be,\" and it is." (Quran 19:35, Saheeh International)

The Arabic phrase rendered "it is not [befitting]" does not merely report that God has not, as a matter of fact, taken a son; it expresses that doing so would be inconsistent with what God is. The verse then explains why: God's characteristic mode of causing anything to exist is a sovereign, effortless command — "Be, and it is" — which is the opposite in every respect of begetting, a process bound up with time, biology, and dependency. A God who creates by command has no need, and no aspect of His nature compatible with becoming anything through a process at all.

This is the theological weight behind the concept of shirk: associating a partner with God, whether by worshipping a created thing alongside Him or by attributing to a created thing what belongs to God alone. Islamic theology treats shirk not as one sin among others but as uniquely serious, because it strikes at the root of everything else: if God's oneness and self-sufficiency are compromised even conceptually, the entire structure of dependence, worship, and accountability built upon that oneness is compromised with it. From this standpoint, calling Jesus "God incarnate" is not simply a mistaken reading of a text; whether or not it was ever intended to add a "partner" alongside God, it functions, in Islamic theological terms, as attributing to the Creator a change, a need, and an origin that only a creature can have — and that, for Tawhid, is among the deepest forms the error can take.

Muslim theologians have historically pressed this argument in exactly the philosophical terms outlined above: God, as the Necessary Existent, cannot acquire a new mode of existing — becoming embodied, being born, hungering, aging, dying — without either ceasing to be necessary in that respect, or the embodied, born, hungering subject not really being God at all. On this reading, no degree of technical refinement to the doctrine, however carefully Christian theologians distinguish person from nature, removes the fact that a single subject is still being asked to be, in reality, both necessary and contingent.

sections.title: The Hypostatic Union and Kenosis: The Church's Own Vocabulary
sections.body:
Christian theology's answer to the coherence objection begins by insisting that the vocabulary of "two natures, one person" and of "self-emptying" is not an invention of philosophers trying to patch an embarrassing doctrine; it is drawn from the New Testament's own language about Christ, and the councils saw their task as guarding that language against distortion rather than adding to it.

The starting point is the Gospel of John's prologue, which identifies Jesus with the eternal Word who was "with God" and "was God" from the beginning, and then states:

"The Word became flesh, and lived among us. We saw his glory, such glory as of the one and only Son of the Father, full of grace and truth." (John 1:14, World English Bible)

Mainstream Christian exegesis reads this verse as asserting exactly the claim under examination: the eternal Word, without ceasing to be what it eternally is, became flesh — took on, in addition to the divine nature it already possessed, a complete human nature, so that "the Word" and "flesh" (a full human existence, body and soul) are now one single subject, the man Jesus of Nazareth. The verb "became" is read by Christian theologians as describing an addition rather than a transformation — the Word does not stop being the Word, or stop being divine, in order to become flesh.

The fullest New Testament statement of how this was understood to work is Paul's hymn in Philippians:

"who, existing in the form of God, didn't consider equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, taking the form of a servant, being made in the likeness of men. And being found in human form, he humbled himself, becoming obedient to death, yes, the death of the cross." (Philippians 2:6-8, World English Bible)

The verb behind "emptied himself" gives the doctrine of kenosis (self-emptying) its name. Two features of this passage matter for the coherence question. First, its structure already assumes the very two-natures logic that the councils would later formalize: the one who empties himself is described as "existing in the form of God" both before and, implicitly, still during the self-emptying — the passage does not say he stopped existing in the form of God, only that he did not cling to the privileges ("equality with God... a thing to be grasped") that status could have claimed, choosing instead to also take "the form of a servant." Second, mainstream, classical Christian interpretation, from the patristic period through Chalcedon and into contemporary academic theology, has consistently read "emptied himself" as self-emptying of glory, honor, and outward divine prerogative, not as the surrender of divine nature or attributes. On this reading, the Word "empties" himself the way a king might set aside his throne, his court, and the outward exercise of his authority to live for a time as a servant among his subjects, without for one moment ceasing to be the king. A minority current within nineteenth- and twentieth-century Protestant theology, sometimes called kenotic Christology, argued that the Son more literally divested himself of certain divine attributes, such as unlimited knowledge, during his earthly life; this view remains a minority position and is not the classical or majority doctrine represented at Chalcedon.

Put together, John 1:14 supplies the assertion — the Word became flesh — and Philippians 2:6-8 supplies the New Testament's own account of the mechanism: not transformation of the divine into the human, and not the divine ceasing in order to make room for the human, but the addition of a complete human nature to the person of the eternal Word, who humbled himself to live within its limitations without those limitations touching what he eternally is as God. The Chalcedonian formula's four negatives — without confusion, without change, without division, without separation — are, on the classical reading, an attempt to state precisely and only what Philippians 2 and John 1 already imply: the two natures are really and permanently united (against Nestorius), yet neither is changed into the other or lost (against Eutyches and Apollinaris).

sections.title: Two Metaphysics of God, Compared
sections.body:
With both positions stated in their strongest form, it becomes possible to see exactly where they meet and where they diverge — and it is not, on close inspection, at the level of a single disputed verse, but at the level of the underlying picture of what God fundamentally is.

The classical Christian defense of coherence turns on a distinction between nature and person. A "nature" answers the question "what kind of thing is this?" (divine or human); a "person" answers the question "who is this?" On this analysis, "God" and "man" are not simple logical contradictories like "married" and "unmarried," where a single subject cannot be both in the same respect at the same time. Rather, they are two complete sets of properties, a divine nature and a human nature, that a single person can, in principle, possess simultaneously without either set canceling the other, provided the properties are correctly assigned: unlimited knowledge belongs to Christ according to his divine nature, and growth in wisdom belongs to him according to his human nature, and it is the same one person, the Word, of whom both are truly said. Contemporary Christian philosophers such as Thomas V. Morris have developed this into a "two-minds" model: the eternal Word possesses a fully divine mind, and in the Incarnation this divine mind also grounds a fully human mind and consciousness, so that the human Jesus can genuinely not know something in his human consciousness while the same person, as the eternal Word, does know it. On this account, no contradiction arises, because the same thing is never affirmed and denied of the same subject in the same respect; it is denied of the human nature and affirmed of the divine nature, both truly belonging to one person.

The Islamic response, at its most rigorous, does not deny that Christian theologians can draw this nature-person distinction consistently as a piece of logic. It denies that the distinction reaches the actual difficulty. The core Islamic objection is not merely that "God" and "man" are incompatible predicates the way "married" and "unmarried" are; it is that necessary existence and contingent existence are not two parallel natures that a single subject can carry the way a person can have both a mind and a body. To exist necessarily is to be the kind of thing whose non-existence is impossible and which depends on nothing for being what it is; to exist contingently is to be the kind of thing that does depend on something else and could have failed to exist. These are not two compatible sets of properties a single subject can bear simultaneously in different respects — they describe two fundamentally different modes of being. On this reading, saying that the person is necessary according to his divine nature and contingent according to his human nature does not resolve the tension; it relocates the same question one level down, since it is still one and the same person, the single subject who is said to have done the assuming, the praying, the hungering, and the dying — a single subject who must, on the Christian account, ultimately depend on nothing as God, and yet be the very subject of a genuinely dependent human life.

There is a second point of contact worth stating carefully, because Christian theology anticipates it directly. Muslim theologians naturally read the claim that God "became" anything as a form of shirk, compromising God's uniqueness by uniting Him with a creature. Christian theology's own answer is that this is a misapplication of the charge: the doctrine of the Trinity, which underwrites the Incarnation, insists it is describing one being in three eternal, co-equal persons, not two beings, a God and a lesser being, joined together — so nothing "partners" with God in the Incarnation; it is God the Son himself, one of the eternal persons of the single Godhead, who assumes a human nature. On this account, the charge of shirk misses its target because there was never a second, independent being for God to be partnered with.

Muslim theologians, in turn, do not regard this response as resolving the difficulty so much as restating it in different terms. From the Islamic side, whether the human nature that is assumed belongs to a second independent being or is created specifically and only for this purpose changes nothing about the core problem: what is assumed — a body, a birth, hunger, growth, suffering, death — are contingent, created realities, and the claim remains that these contingent realities become directly, personally, and permanently predicable of the very subject who is also, without qualification, the necessary, uncreated God. Whether this is technically called shirk or something else, Muslim theologians hold that ascribing createdness and change to God's own person, by whatever route, contradicts Tawhid's requirement that God be free of both.

What this comparison shows is that neither side's argument rests on a simple failure to understand the other's position. The Christian two-natures defense is a genuine, technically serious answer to the objection that God and man are incompatible predicates. The Islamic rejoinder is a genuine, technically serious observation that the deeper modal distinction, necessary versus contingent existence, is not obviously dissolved by the nature-person move, however carefully it is stated. Both arguments have been refined for centuries without either side conceding that the other has simply missed the point.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
A few misunderstandings recur often enough on both sides to deserve direct treatment.

First: "Christian doctrine teaches that God stopped being God, or was somehow reduced, in order to become a man." Classical Christian theology denies this explicitly. The Chalcedonian Definition's phrase "without change" exists precisely to rule out this reading. On the classical account, the eternal Word remains, without interruption or diminishment, fully God throughout the Incarnation; what changes is that a complete human nature is additionally united to his person. Even the kenosis of Philippians 2 is read by mainstream theology as an emptying of status and prerogative, not of divine nature.

Second: "Muslims object to the Incarnation only because they misunderstand it as belief in three gods, so the objection dissolves once the Trinity is properly explained." This is not accurate, and the comparison above shows why: the transcendence argument from Tawhid, that necessary existence and contingent existence cannot be predicated of one and the same subject, applies with equal force even to a strictly unitarian claim that God became a man, with no Trinity involved at all. The objections to the Incarnation and to the Trinity are related in Islamic theology, since both are read as compromising God's absolute oneness and self-sufficiency, but they are logically distinct arguments, and clarifying the Trinity does not, on the Islamic view, remove the separate difficulty about becoming.

Third: "Kenosis means Jesus gave up unlimited knowledge or other divine attributes while on earth, so he was not fully God during his earthly life." This describes a minority, historically later position, sometimes called kenotic Christology, rather than the mainstream, classical doctrine. The Chalcedonian and patristic consensus is that the divine nature retained all its attributes without interruption throughout the Incarnation; any apparent limitation, such as Mark 13:32's statement that the Son does not know the hour, is attributed by classical theology to the human nature and its mode of knowing, not to any diminishment of the divine nature itself.

Fourth: "The Islamic objection to the Incarnation amounts to saying Christian theologians have simply failed to notice an obvious contradiction in their own doctrine." This misreads the seriousness of both traditions' engagement with the question. Muslim theologians across many centuries, including rigorous engagement with Christian philosophical vocabulary itself, have argued that the difficulty survives every refinement Christian doctrine offers, precisely because they take the Christian formulations seriously enough to examine them in detail rather than dismiss them out of hand. Christian philosophers, for their part, have continued to refine the coherence defense, the two-minds model being one recent example, precisely because they recognize the objection as a genuine philosophical challenge deserving a genuine answer, not a rhetorical objection to be waved away.

sections.title: Two Starting Points About God
sections.body:
Every argument eventually reaches bedrock — a premise from which the argument proceeds but which is not itself derived from something more basic. The disagreement over the Incarnation reaches its bedrock quickly, and it is worth naming plainly rather than treating either tradition as having simply blundered.

Classical Christian theology holds that God's transcendence, rightly understood, is compatible with a free, personal act in which the eternal Word assumes, without loss, without change, and without dilution, a complete human nature into personal union with himself, remaining, in that very act, exactly as much God as before. On this premise, the Incarnation is the deepest possible expression of a God who is love, self-giving, and personal, rather than a compromise of His transcendence.

Islamic theology, grounded in Tawhid, holds that God's transcendence categorically excludes any such becoming, full stop, not because such an act would be difficult or unlikely, but because a God who could unite Himself with created, contingent, changing reality in this way would not be the absolutely self-sufficient, unbegotten, unbegetting God that Tawhid describes in the first place. On this premise, the very possibility considered by Christian doctrine is ruled out before any argument about Jesus in particular even begins.

Neither position is being asserted here to be self-evidently absurd by careful thinkers on the other side; each is the considered conclusion of a long, sophisticated theological and philosophical tradition reasoning from its own starting axioms about the nature of God. Readers who want to understand why each side starts where it does are encouraged to read this library's companion articles, What Is Tawhid? and What Is the Trinity?, which set out each tradition's positive doctrine of God on its own terms — the doctrines from which everything in this article ultimately follows.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Is this article asking whether Jesus claimed to be God?
A: No. That historical and textual question is addressed in this library's companion article, Did Jesus Claim to Be God? This article asks a separate, philosophical question: assuming Christian doctrine is correctly understood, is the claim that God became man even a coherent one to make about anything called "God"?

Q: What is the hypostatic union?
A: It is the classical Christian doctrine, defined at the Council of Chalcedon in 451 CE, that in the person of Jesus Christ, one person exists in two complete natures, fully divine and fully human, "without confusion, without change, without division, without separation."

Q: What does kenosis mean, and does it mean Jesus stopped being fully God?
A: Kenosis, from the Greek verb in Philippians 2:7 meaning "emptied," refers to Christ's self-emptying in taking the form of a servant. In mainstream Christian theology this means Christ set aside the outward privileges and glory of his divine status, not that he surrendered any divine attribute or ceased, even temporarily, to be fully God.

Q: Why does Islam treat the Incarnation as more than an unproven claim?
A: Because Tawhid holds that God's nature, uncreated, unbegotten, unchanging, and without need, categorically excludes union with anything created, contingent, or changing. On this view, the Incarnation does not merely lack sufficient evidence; it attempts to unite two modes of existence, necessary and contingent, that cannot be united in one subject.

Q: Does clarifying that the Trinity is not tritheism resolve the Islamic objection to the Incarnation?
A: Not fully. The objection that necessary and contingent existence cannot belong to one subject applies whether one, two, or three divine persons are involved. Christian theology's clarification that the Trinity is one being in three persons answers the separate charge of worshipping multiple gods, but the distinct question of whether God can become anything at all remains open on Islamic premises.

Q: Does either tradition consider the other's position simply mistaken through carelessness?
A: No. Both the classical Christian defense of the Incarnation's coherence and the Islamic objection from Tawhid are the product of centuries of sophisticated theological and philosophical refinement. The disagreement traces to different starting premises about what God's nature allows, not to an oversight on either side.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The following works allow readers to go deeper into both the philosophical coherence question and its theological history, from Christian, historical, and Muslim perspectives.

Thomas V. Morris — The Logic of God Incarnate offers a rigorous, contemporary analytic-philosophy defense of the two-natures doctrine's coherence, including the two-minds model discussed in this article's comparison section. Aloys Grillmeier — Christ in Christian Tradition: From the Apostolic Age to Chalcedon is the standard scholarly history of how the doctrine developed through the early councils, tracing each error the Church corrected on the way to Chalcedon. Richard Swinburne — The Christian God presents a leading analytic philosopher's case for the coherence of the Trinity and Incarnation together, representing a strong contemporary philosophical defense. Ibn Taymiyyah — Al-Jawab al-Sahih li-man Baddala Din al-Masih ("The Correct Answer to Those Who Have Corrupted the Religion of Christ") is a major classical Muslim theological response to Christian doctrine, engaging the Incarnation and Trinity in detail from within Islamic philosophical categories, included here so readers can weigh the Islamic argument in its own most developed form.

But of that day or that hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father.
but emptied himself, taking the form of a servant, being made in the likeness of men.

faq.question: Is this article asking whether Jesus claimed to be God?
faq.answer: No. That historical and textual question is addressed in this library's companion article, Did Jesus Claim to Be God? This article asks a separate, philosophical question: assuming Christian doctrine is correctly understood, is the claim that God became man even a coherent one to make about anything called "God"?

faq.question: What is the hypostatic union?
faq.answer: It is the classical Christian doctrine, defined at the Council of Chalcedon in 451 CE, that in the person of Jesus Christ, one person exists in two complete natures, fully divine and fully human, "without confusion, without change, without division, without separation."

faq.question: What does kenosis mean, and does it mean Jesus stopped being fully God?
faq.answer: Kenosis, from the Greek verb in Philippians 2:7 meaning "emptied," refers to Christ's self-emptying in taking the form of a servant. In mainstream Christian theology this means Christ set aside the outward privileges and glory of his divine status, not that he surrendered any divine attribute or ceased, even temporarily, to be fully God.

faq.question: Why does Islam treat the Incarnation as more than an unproven claim?
faq.answer: Because Tawhid holds that God's nature, uncreated, unbegotten, unchanging, and without need, categorically excludes union with anything created, contingent, or changing. On this view, the Incarnation does not merely lack sufficient evidence; it attempts to unite two modes of existence, necessary and contingent, that cannot be united in one subject.

faq.question: Does clarifying that the Trinity is not tritheism resolve the Islamic objection to the Incarnation?
faq.answer: Not fully. The objection that necessary and contingent existence cannot belong to one subject applies whether one, two, or three divine persons are involved. Christian theology's clarification that the Trinity is one being in three persons answers the separate charge of worshipping multiple gods, but the distinct question of whether God can become anything at all remains open on Islamic premises.

faq.question: Does either tradition consider the other's position simply mistaken through carelessness?
faq.answer: No. Both the classical Christian defense of the Incarnation's coherence and the Islamic objection from Tawhid are the product of centuries of sophisticated theological and philosophical refinement. The disagreement traces to different starting premises about what God's nature allows, not to an oversight on either side.

furtherReading.title: The Logic of God Incarnate
furtherReading.author: Thomas V. Morris
furtherReading.note: A rigorous analytic-philosophy defense of the logical coherence of the two-natures doctrine, including the two-minds model discussed in this article.

furtherReading.title: Christ in Christian Tradition: From the Apostolic Age to Chalcedon
furtherReading.author: Aloys Grillmeier
furtherReading.note: The standard scholarly history of how the doctrine of Christ's two natures developed through the early councils, including Nicaea, Ephesus, and Chalcedon.

furtherReading.title: The Christian God
furtherReading.author: Richard Swinburne
furtherReading.note: A leading analytic philosopher of religion's defense of the coherence of the Trinity and Incarnation, useful for readers who want the strongest contemporary philosophical case.

furtherReading.title: Al-Jawab al-Sahih li-man Baddala Din al-Masih (The Correct Answer to Those Who Have Corrupted the Religion of Christ)
furtherReading.author: Ibn Taymiyyah
furtherReading.note: A major classical Muslim theological engagement with Christian doctrines, including the Incarnation and Trinity, argued from within Islamic philosophical categories.
